body_txt: The big box store variation of the crappy fast food McJob , the low-level office job or the dreaded kids' party entertainer job . The store is filled to brim with merchandise reaching up to the fluorescent light ceiling, annoying consumption-crazed customers and of course, dozens of overworked, underpaid disgruntled employees. This is where dreams of a successful career go to die, right into the clearance section. The New Job Episode will often focus on one character working in such an environment (if he doesn't become a Burger Fool ) and then subsequently finding out being a grocery store or local Wal-Mart employee is not as easy as it looks. Alternatively, the fiction will sometimes focus on those poor souls that have already been working there for years (such as in Clerks , Employee of the Month , or Superstore ) and will give the viewer an inside look at life behind the counter. A prolonged period of working one of these jobs may result in an Apathetic Clerk . Either way, this is Truth in Television . Jobs like this are certainly no fun. Between the obnoxious customers who think "the customer is always right" is Serious Business , the lazy and incompetent co-workers dumping all their tasks on the interns and temps who will end up having to take the fall for them, the horrible music (especially during the holiday season), the long hours consisting of standing on increasingly-blistered feet , the pressure to work harder from impatient customers (and co-workers), and the tyrannical management, quitting or getting fired is sometimes the only way out . In the U.S. and a sizeable portion of Europe, since a lot of manufacturing has been moved overseas, retail and hospitality have become the standard "blue collar" jobs. Unfortunately, with the prevailing retail apocalypse resulting in numerous retail chains downsizing seriously or going out of business altogether, this job is getting more precarious as well. If the store is making people's lives miserable outside the store as well, it's a Predatory Business . Can also be a Black Company , especially when set in Japan. Compare Too Proud for Lowly Work , when the worst thing about the job is it's beneath the character's sense of vanity. When a character is worn down by a violent or upsetting job rather than a monotonously mundane one, see Burnout by Traumatic Job . Has nothing to do with literally sucking a soul .
